title: Chapter 25
text: Chapter Twenty - Two R onin watched as the skin on his right leg was carefully cut and peeled away. The machine’s larger arms lowered, and numerous attachments went to work. Within a minute, everything below his right knee had been removed. He didn’t power off the sensors in the area. The pain was intense, but fleeting; a brief flicker of life. Such sensations were both more intense and longer-lasting for humans . After his leg was repaired and reattached, the machine moved upward, filling in the holes in his casing and making minor repairs to his damaged internals. Finally, it loomed over his head, tiny arms cutting away the shrapnel embedded in his left optic. Before repairs were made, it switched off his optical input . He occupied himself by sifting through his memory. He replayed Lara’s dance, reviewed their conversations and their arguments, lamented the amount of time he’d spent apart from her. Echoes of what he’d experienced during their numerous couplings skittered across his electrodes, lighting up skin that was no longer present . More than anything else, he replayed her confession of love . The attendant directed Ronin to the epidermal synthesizer once his optics were online. He continued his reveries in the chamber . He hadn’t told Lara he loved her. Hadn’t known how, though the words were so simple; it would have taken less than one and a half seconds of speech. She’d given no indication that she was bothered by it, but that didn’t make it all right . When the repairs were complete, he dressed — the shirt Lara made was more comfortable against his skin than anything had ever been, apart from Lara herself — and thanked the attendant. He’d been away from home for twelve hours and thirty-five minutes, and wanted nothing more than to be back with her. To tell her that he loved her , too . An itch pulsed across his cheek. He ignored it . “Good to see you fully repaired,” Mercy said pleasantly . Ronin glanced at her and halted abruptly. Her smile was gone, belying her tone . “What’s wrong?” he asked . “Nothing.” Her optics flicked to the entrance. “It’s just that… he came looking for you, while you were in there .” “He want me to go see him ?” “No.” She dropped her gaze and pressed her pink lips together. “He’s been waiting outside for the last three hours and forty-two minutes .” Ronin turned his head to the doors. Between the contrasting lighting and the reflections on the glass, the details were blurry, but he counted five distinct figures standing about thirty feet from the entrance . There were undoubtedly other exits from the building, but Ronin had only been in the lobby and the repair room. He dismissed the possibility. Such an escape would only lead Warlord to Ronin’s residence, and he didn’t want this scar-faced bot anywhere near Lara . He walked toward them, ceasing his processors’ attempted speculation — Warlord’s whims seemed as difficult to predict as Lara’s. The doors whirred open, their sound indicating an inevitable motor failure sometime in the future . If the group of bots had been conversing, their words fell silent when Ronin stepped outside. They all looked at him as he approached. Warlord stood at the center of the group, unassumingly average in height, build, and features compared to his companions, save for the sutured gash on his cheek. Even though the gearheads displayed far more bare metal, Warlord’s damage was somehow more imposing . Ronin stopped several feet away . Should have brought the pistol . A single round in Warlord’s optic might have been enough to short his system entirely . “Dustwalker. Looking better than you did when you came in last night,” Warlord said, expression neutral . “Feeling it, too.” Ronin met the optics of each of the gearheads before settling his focus on Warlord again. “Just here to make sure I’m repaired ?” “You push boundaries, dustwalker. Maybe that’s good, out there. Maybe that’s why you’re so productive. But we’ve been over this already .” “You know the condition I was in when I came back, and the sun was still up .” “I’ve dealt with your type. I understand you. I told you that my rules are to be obeyed, didn’t I? I don’t think my memory’s been corrupted in the last month. Maybe you can refresh me, so we can be sure .” “Your bots let me through without a word . They — ” “You know that’s not what this is about!” Warlord growled, jabbing a finger at Ronin and stepping forward. “You’re smarter than that, dustwalker. Or you think you are, anyway .” Ronin’s processors blazed through data, searching every moment he’d spent in Cheyenne. There weren’t many conversations with Warlord to go over, but there were always rules, rarely presented with clarity . Warlord pursed his lips and turned away, pacing past the gearheads. “Did you think we wouldn’t notice, Ronin ?” “ Notice what ?” “You’ve been purchasing a great deal of food, considering you can’t eat. Extra clothing in smaller sizes. You’ve spent more idle time in Cheyenne over the last two weeks than you have since you first came here .” Realization hammered into Ronin’s mind, battering his CPU into overload. There were too many possibilities to assess. Too many things they might have done to her . “You brought a human through my wall. None of my people have seen her leave. That means you’ve been keeping her .” The gearheads shifted their stances subtly, spreading their feet and squaring their shoulders. Their overly-grim expressions might have been comical in any other situation. What could they do? Any pain Ronin suffered at their hands would be done in an instant, relegated to the depths of his memory . The same wouldn’t hold true for Lara . His processors superimposed her face on Tabitha’s broken body. Had some of these gearheads taken part in that brutal beating? Which of them had harmed Lara’s only family, had ensured that Tabitha’s suffering was deep and lasting before her eventual death ? “If I had a woman, why would it be a problem? I’ve seen humans on the arms of bots within your walls, right in the faces of your people .” “I’m starting to wonder if you’ll ever learn, dustwalker. And that’s a damned shame, because bots of your talent are a boon to this community.” Warlord walked up to Ronin, narrowing his eyes, and lowered his voice. “Nothing happens in Cheyenne without my say so. Especially not one of those parasites taking up residence behind my wall. Their place is out there, in the world they destroyed .” “So, I’m expected to know your will in all things, without being told?” The question was a risk, but he nearly opened his mouth to say more. They couldn’t do much to him — on or off — but there was a lot they could do to Lara . “Boulder. Northside.” Warlord motioned to a pair of gearheads; the blocky one, Northside, had been guarding the gate to the Bot District the night Ronin brought Lara inside . Each of the bots grabbed one of Ronin’s arms and pushed. He locked himself in place, exerting force against them. Warlord didn’t look away, his expression unchanging as he gestured the two remaining gearheads forward . More hands clamped down on Ronin, and within moments, they swept his legs out from beneath him. He hit the pavement hard, and gearheads knelt on his limbs before he could rise . Warlord approached slowly, as though he had nothing better to do, nowhere else to be, and stopped over Ronin’s head . Ronin poured excess power into his actuators and struggled to sit up. The gearheads swayed, quickly adjusting their weight to press him down . Warlord eased into a crouch. “You’re expected, Ronin , to get rid of that worthless meatbag. You’ve done well for us here, brought in valuable resources…so I’m willing to extend you my generosity this one, last time. There’s fight in you, and I can admire that…but it doesn’t mean shit if you try to fight me. Get rid of the meat, or I will. And then we’ll tear you apart, one piece at a time .” Ronin stared up at Warlord’s face, fury raging inside him. Reason said to agree politely and be done with it; deception and charm were the proper way to extricate himself from this situation. But whatever Lara had awoken within him — fire, emotion, love — couldn’t accept the threat with a nod and a fake smile, couldn’t bear the thought of allowing this to happen without protest . “You can prosper with us, or become the scrap that fuels us. Don’t ever say I didn’t give you the choice.” Warlord rose and walked away, boots thumping over the pavement. The gearheads delayed for fifteen seconds before following him . Ronin sat up and watched the group leave, their shadows stretching across the road in the setting sun. They turned east when they reached the main street; they weren’t going to Ronin’s residence, at least not directly . But had they already been there? Had they already done Lara harm ? He pushed up to his feet and ran. He should have listened to her, should have taken her away from here sooner. Shouldn’t have gone on his last run. What had that delay cost them ? The front door was locked. He forced it open, wood splintering, and it slammed hard into the wall . “ Lara !” Striding into the main room, he swept his optics over it. There was precious little time to gather their supplies, and he’d have to prioritize based on her needs. Food, water, clothing. The Dust would not be forgiving to her . Footsteps on the stairs. He snapped his head to the side as Lara ran down . “What? What’s wrong?” she asked, wide - eyed . For once, he was glad to see her clothed. Her ring hung around her neck on a thick piece of twine. Ronin wished she could wear it on her finger, but it was too obvious a target . “We need to go .” “Wasn’t that the plan already ?” “Now.” He moved past her, climbing the steps three at a time . Lara hurried behind him. “What do you mean, now? It’s still light outside, and we haven’t traded anything .” He entered his bedroom. She’d already packed his rucksack and laid out their folded clothing on the bed. His tools were arranged in his belt pouches, the handgun in its holster nearby. He went directly to the closet, pulling down the spare bags from the upper shelf and tearing two coats from their hangers . “What the hell’s going on, Ronin ?” “We need to leave,” he replied . “You said that already .” “He knows you’re here, Lara.” He turned to face her. The color had drained from her face. “He doesn’t know it’s you , I don’t think, but that doesn’t matter. We have to gather everything that’s essential, and get out of here .” He held up the smaller of the two coats to compare it to her body. It was still much too large, but it would be her only real protection from the unforgiving weather . “Shouldn’t we at least wait until dark?” She took the coat from him and hugged it to her chest . “No.” Swinging the other coat on, he returned to the bed and picked up the pistol, offering it to Lara grip-first. “It needs to be now . We’ll cut through the backyards until we have to cross the street, then swing south, through the slums until we’re out of sight of the wall. Then we’ll cut west .” “What about the guards ?” “They’re only posted at the gates, most of the time .” “And we’re going over the wall .” He nodded and raised his hand slightly. “ Take it .” She closed her fingers around the pistol’s grip and stared at it. “I’ll…go get ready .” As she walked away, Ronin put his hand on her upper arm, turning her toward him. Stepping close, he cradled her face in his hands and pressed his forehead against hers. His immense relief at seeing her unharmed wouldn’t mean anything until Cheyenne was far behind him, but he could relish a single moment of togetherness, of her beauty . “I’ll keep you safe, Lara Brooks .” She smiled, her breath tickling his skin, and placed a hand on his forearm. “ I know .” “Pack as much food as you can. Focus on anything that’ll last more than a day or two. And fill as many containers as you can find with water .” “Okay.” Lara withdrew, coat still draped over one arm, and left the room . He didn’t turn to his gear until she reached the staircase and exited his field of vision . * * * “R eady?” Ronin asked . Lara tilted her head back to stare up at the wall. She’d only seen it from the outside, where the mismatched pieces somehow came together to form an almost-sheer face. It couldn’t be called pretty by any stretch, but this side was far worse. There’d been no attempt at uniformity; pieces of metal sheeting and piping jutted out of it in some places, wooden slats and chunks of concrete in others . Seeing it so close, with light still in the sky, made the rest of the Bot District feel uglier. Like all its cleanliness was just a false front . After cutting through alleys and backyards to avoid gearheads, this was supposed to be the easy part. But from here, the wall seemed likely to collapse and crush her to death . “ Ready .” Ronin dropped to a knee and laced his fingers together. “I’ll pass up your bag, once you’re on top .” She nodded, setting her pack down and placing her foot on his hands. As he stood, she steadied herself by holding his shoulders. When he shifted a hand beneath each of her feet, she used the wall for stability, trusting her own balance less than she trusted him . The top of the wall was at her waist. She found handholds and lifted a leg over it, grateful for the padding of the coat as a metal edge pressed against her stomach. Within a few seconds, she was straddling the wall . Ronin handed the bag up. She took it and settled it between her thighs. After checking the area behind him, he passed her the rifle, which she laid over the bag and held securely in place. Then, despite carrying two packs full of scrap, his belt of tools, their ammunition, and another bag full of water containers, he climbed with little apparent difficulty . The wall groaned as he neared the top. Lara sucked in a breath, hands tightening on the rifle . He pulled himself up, a piece of concrete breaking off his last foothold. It landed in the dirt below with a dull thump . His eyes met hers for a moment, his lips lifting in a little smile, and then he searched the area again. Lara did the same; she’d never seen the human shacks from this angle. The contrast between the opposite sides of the wall was stark . Within the barrier, everything was clean and deceptively alive. Orderly and precise. Green trees along the streets, electric lamps only now flickering on, well-maintained rooftops. Beyond the wall, the landscape was bleak, a smattering of browns and yellows. The only plant life was the scrub grass poking up through the dust. Clumped together with no pattern, the human shacks were small, rickety, filthy things . The sun was slipping away on the western horizon. It would be dark soon, and they’d be out there, in the shadows … “Looks clear.” Ronin leapt down, dust swirling up around his boots as he landed. He held his arms up toward Lara . She lowered the rifle, and once he had it strapped over his shoulder, she passed down her bag. Ronin set it at his feet and raised his arms once more. Without hesitation, Lara swung her other leg over the top of the wall and pushed off . He caught her easily, holding her against him . “Hard part’s done,” he said, finally . “Seemed simple enough,” she replied, smirking . “Chance worked in our favor. We just need to pass through there,” Ronin tipped his head toward the shacks she used to call home, “and we’ll be in Cheyenne no more.” He set her gently on her feet and bent to retrieve her bag . She shrugged it on and adjusted the straps; it wasn’t terribly heavy, but she’d have to carry it a long way. “Should be easy. Most everyone beds down early .” Ronin nodded, swept his head from side to side in another search, and walked, gravel crunching beneath his boots. Lara adjusted her scarf, fashioning it into a makeshift hood, and followed across the no-man’s land between the wall and the human settlement . It would be easy to forget the danger, now that she was on the other side of the wall again, but they weren’t clear yet. Not until the lights of Cheyenne were a memory, lost beyond the horizon . Quiet apart from their footfalls, they followed the old trail. She’d walked it before, more times than she could count, but it had never felt this way. The last time, she’d been filled with uncertainty and fear. She hadn’t known Ronin when he led her to the Market. Hadn’t known if she’d be okay, if she’d ever find her sister, if she’d survive the night with the strange bot who’d stalked her through the ruins. It was a journey into the unknown . This was, too, but she wasn’t alone now. Ronin was familiar. She loved him, trusted him, would go anywhere with him . Her eyes drifted to her old shack as they neared it. Light spilled through the gaps around the closed door. Oddly, Lara wasn’t upset about someone living there. She and Tabitha had built it, together, but it was never anything more than shelter. Home had been with Tabitha…and now it was with Ronin . “I wonder how long it took .” “How long what took?” Ronin glanced over his shoulder . “For someone to notice I was gone .” He swung his gaze to the shack. “In a way, you’re helping someone else who was in need. Just like Tabitha helped you .” “Yeah,” she said, eyes lingering on the structure while they walked past. When it was behind her, she settled her attention on Ronin’s back . “ Yeah .” They pressed on. Everything was familiar to her, even in the deepening shadows. These were the roads she’d taken to fetch water, to head out to the ruins, to visit Gary and Kate or the other people she sometimes traded with. The few people still outside cast them wary glances. If anyone recognized Lara, no one spoke to her . Soon, the shacks were behind them. Had the place always been so small? Ronin led her up a gradual embankment. At the top, it leveled off on a wide, flat road that stretched as far as she could see to the east and west. The grass and weeds growing through the cracked, mostly-buried pavement swayed in the wind. Only a hint of orange remained on the horizon, now, the rest of the sky black . Ronin stopped at the edge of the road and turned to look north. Lara stood beside him, wind tugging at her scarf and clothing. The coat Ronin gave her blocked the worst of it. She followed his gaze to Cheyenne. The Bot District’s lights were bright, casting a yellow-tinted glow in the hazy sky . “I’m sorry to leave, only because of what that place could have been,” Ronin said . “What do you think it could have been ?” “A place with a promising future. A place worth staying in.” He turned to her, shadows deep on his face. “A place where we wouldn’t have to worry. Where we could just…live. Not just us, but everyone .” Lara took his hand, lacing her fingers with his. “Bots and humans have never lived together .” His hand was warm, fingers firm but not ungentle. “I don’t think that’s true .” She furrowed her brow and gazed up at him. “What do you mean ?” “Our peoples have forgotten, but the way it is in Cheyenne isn’t the way it always was. Something changed it, a long time ago …” “If all those houses are like yours, they have things in them for humans. Toilets and beds and boxes to keep food cold. Humans lived in those houses .” “Yes. And in the Clinic, there are dozens of rooms with signs pertaining to human care .” A strange feeling spread through her, some mix of elation — she’d been right! — and dread. If people had lived in that part of Cheyenne…why didn’t they anymore ? “What about the man who lived in your attic ?” “He probably lived in the house. People lived in all those houses. Until…” He shook his head, and even in the failing light, she saw the tic of his jaw. “Doesn’t matter .” “It does matter,” she insisted. “Until what, Ronin ?” “I will tell you, Lara, but not until there are many miles between us and this place .” “You can tell me now. I deserve to know, don’t I ?” He turned his head and settled his unblinking gaze on her. The wind filled in his silence for a long time . “The journal you found in the attic talks about what happened in Cheyenne, a long time ago. It hints at there having been some terrible war, but the man did not write much about that. He recorded the events unfolding in town. Right outside that window .” Lara squeezed his hand. She thought she knew, somehow, what he was going to tell her, but she wanted to hear it from him . “Warlord was there. I don’t think he was calling himself that yet…but he was there. He forced the humans out of their homes and started building his wall, claiming Cheyenne as his own, and executed those who disobeyed. Many of them in the park .” Her stomach sank, twisting in on itself. All that peaceful grass, the vibrant trees, all grown where people had been murdered ? “The humans rallied together to fight for their homes, to fight for whatever was left of their lives. But they couldn’t stand against him. They were slaughtered in the streets. After that, he and his bots went from one house to the next, killing every human they found .” “ Fuck ,” was all she could manage . “It was a very long time ago, I think. Clearly, his value of humans has not much changed .” “It doesn’t surprise me, not really. Still makes me sick…but everything about him does that. I just…what the hell did those people do to deserve that? What have any of us done ?” “Cheyenne is capable of providing for your every need and allowing you a comfortable life,” Ronin said. He resumed walking, still holding her hand, and she fell into step beside him. “But it will not, so long as he is there .” The humans rallied together to fight for their homes, to fight for whatever was left of their lives. But they couldn’t stand against him. They were slaughtered in the street . Ronin’s words echoed through her mind. People were still being slaughtered, one at a time, for Warlord’s amusement. They lived in constant fear and hopelessness. And here she was, leaving without a word, getting herself to someplace safer . She frowned, guilt heavy in her stomach . “Well…I hope you have some more pleasant stories to tell, if we’re going to be traveling for a while .” Seeming to know where her mind dwelled, Ronin lifted her hand and kissed it. “Perhaps we’ll find ourselves in a comfortable place to camp, soon enough, so I may suitably distract you .” She stared at him, shaking her head. “That is such a man thing to say .” “I can’t tell if I’ve offended you or not .” “That’s another man thing .” “Perhaps I can take some comfort, then, in human males being as clueless about women as bots are .” “We’re not really that hard to figure out,” she said, grinning despite everything . His only answer was an incredulous scoff . Whenever they stopped, she knew the events of the last few days would crash down upon her, but for now she could find some contentment with Ronin .
